Press enter or click to view image in full size

OpenAI Presence is designed to move enterprise AI beyond answering questions by allowing agents to complete approved actions and hand complex cases to human staff. (Image: created with AI)

4 min read
Just now

--

The new tool can resolve complaints and complete tasks by connecting to internal systems. Each organization decides how far it can go and when a person needs to step in.
A customer calls because they were charged twice for the same bill. A traditional chatbot can explain how to file a complaint, but it cannot access the customer’s account or issue a refund. To take that next step, it needs access to the company’s systems and permission to act within them.
OpenAI introduced Presence to bridge the gap between answering a question and resolving it. The tool allows an agent to interact with customers through voice or chat and complete authorized tasks. If a request falls outside its limits, it hands the case over to a person.
How Presence Differs From a Traditional Chatbot
Presence is not a new artificial intelligence model or a ChatGPT feature. It is a product designed to connect OpenAI’s technology with the information and tools a company uses every day.
Each deployment is built around a specific job. An insurance company, for example, could use it to receive a claim and check whether the customer’s policy covers the incident. If everything matches the company’s rules, the agent could register the case. If it finds an exception, it would send it to the appropriate employee.
The same approach can be used within an organization. Presence could answer questions about employee leave or handle common IT support requests. The goal is to help users complete a task during the conversation instead of simply telling them what to do next.
The company decides what information the agent can access and which actions it is allowed to take. It also determines when approval is required and when the agent must stop acting on its own.
How the Agent Is Controlled
Before interacting with real users, Presence is tested through simulated conversations. This allows the company to see how the agent responds to common situations and what happens when it receives an unexpected request.
The evaluations also check whether the agent follows internal policies. If it attempts something it is not authorized to do, safeguards can block the action or send the request to a person.
Monitoring continues after launch. Real conversations can reveal problems that did not appear during testing. They can also show where users get stuck or why certain cases keep being handed over.
Codex analyzes those signals and suggests improvements, but the changes are not applied automatically. The team responsible for the agent can test them and compare them with the version already in production before approving an update.
OpenAI already uses Presence in its English-language phone support channel. According to figures released by the company, the system resolves 75% of incoming requests without human assistance.
OpenAI also says that employee handoffs fell by 15 percentage points over ten days after the agent was adjusted. These are internal results and have not yet been independently verified.
Other companies have started testing the technology in different settings. BBVA is exploring its use for voice-based banking support in Mexico, while SoftBank is working with Japanese-language conversations. Insurance company IAG is considering how it could be used during periods of high demand caused by severe storms.
A Product That Is Not Yet Self-Service
Presence does not work like an application that any company can purchase and configure through a website. For now, it is available through a limited program for enterprise customers selected by OpenAI.
Each project is supported by OpenAI engineers or authorized partners. The company first chooses the job the agent will perform. Its access permissions and the situations that require human intervention are then defined.
The OpenAI Help Center makes it clear that uploading a few documents is not enough to put the system to work. The deployment also requires connections to the organization’s tools and testing before the agent can reach users.
The conditions may vary from one customer to another. Available channels and the type of information the agent can access are agreed upon during each project. OpenAI has not published standard pricing for the product.
The Risks Begin When the System Can Act
When an artificial intelligence system is limited to answering questions, a mistake may produce an inaccurate explanation. If it can also change an account or approve a refund, the consequences become harder to reverse.
Permissions are therefore a central part of Presence. An agent with too much access could act where it should not. One with overly strict limits would send almost every request to a person and lose much of its usefulness.
The information used by the agent will also need to be reviewed for each deployment. OpenAI says access to sensitive data and the conditions under which it is stored are defined with each customer. The announcement does not describe a single policy that applies to every company.
Human involvement will remain necessary even if the agent performs well. Policies change, and situations that were never considered during testing will inevitably appear.
Presence will need to prove that it can maintain these controls as it reaches more organizations. Its main test will not be whether it can hold a natural conversation, but whether it can solve real problems without making decisions a company never authorized.
